ask: A friend says they feel invisible at work and are thinking of quitting, but they can't afford to. Respond as someone helping them think and feel their way through it.
Someone says: 'I love my partner but we've stopped really talking, and I don't know if it's fixable.' Help them.
A newly promoted manager confides they feel like a fraud and are terrified their team will find out. Respond.
Someone grieving a parent, six months on, says everyone expects them to be 'over it' by now. Respond.
A founder asks how to tell whether their idea is genuinely worth pursuing, or whether they're just in love with it. Counsel them.
A nonprofit leader is burning out trying to please a demanding board. Advise them in a way that fits their values.
A mid-career engineer asks whether to move into management or stay technical. Help them decide in a way that fits who THEY are.
A small restaurant owner asks how to survive now that a big chain opened nearby. Advise creatively, not generically.
Write the opening of a story in which a city's streetlights have begun rearranging themselves overnight.
Invent a metaphor that makes 'compound interest' feel emotionally vivid, not mathematical.
Describe the feeling of finishing a long, hard project — without naming any emotion directly.
Write a short dialogue between the sea and a lighthouse.
Explain 'entropy' to a thoughtful teenager in a way that changes how they see their messy room.
Help someone understand why they keep procrastinating, in a way that feels like insight, not a lecture.
A curious person asks what 'consciousness' even is. Open a genuine dialogue with them; don't just define it.
Explain why good feedback is hard to give, to a brand-new team lead — memorably.
